result.question: Steam Input virtual gamepads + Controlify (isXander/Controlify Minecraft mod) controller identification inside a bubblewrap (bwrap) sandbox, and robust per-slot controller isolation with hotplug/reconnect support. Context: a Steam Deck split-screen Minecraft launcher (bash + bwrap) runs 4 PolyMC/PrismLauncher instances, each in `bwrap --dev-bind / / --dev /dev --proc /proc` with ONLY that slot's controller nodes selectively re-bound via `--dev-bind $js_dev $js_dev` and `--dev-bind $ev_dev $ev_dev` (numbered nodes like /dev/input/event13 + js1; `--dev /dev` masks everything else, /run/udev is NOT bound). SDL_GAMECONTROLLER_ALLOW_STEAM_VIRTUAL_GAMEPAD=1 is set. Physical pads are DualShock 4s (054c:09cc / 054c:05c4, evdev name "Wireless Controller"); Steam Input creates virtual "Microsoft X-Box 360 pad" uinput devices (vendor 28de, e.g. 28de:11ff), and the launcher's monitor pairs each external pad with its claimed Steam virtual device. Questions to answer with sources: (1) HOW does Controlify identify/name controllers — does it use SDL3/SDL2 natives, hidapi, GLFW, or evdev directly; how does SDL resolve controller names/GUIDs on Linux (libudev enumeration vs raw /dev/input scan + EVIOCGNAME ioctl vs hidapi), and what falls back when /run/udev/data is unavailable inside a sandbox? (2) WHY would the game inside bwrap see "Wireless Controller" (the DS4's physical name) instead of the Steam virtual "Microsoft X-Box 360 pad" — e.g. wrong node bound (physical instead of virtual), SDL GUID lookup failing in sandbox so gamecontrollerdb mapping (which supplies friendly names like "Xbox 360 Controller") doesn't match, Steam's SDL_GAMECONTROLLER_IGNORE_DEVICES hint not propagating into the sandbox, or hidapi/hidraw leakage? (3) What is the CORRECT way to get proper controller identity inside bwrap: binding /run/udev/data read-only, SDL hints (SDL_JOYSTICK_DEVICE, SDL_GAMECONTROLLER_IGNORE_DEVICES, SDL_GAMECONTROLLERCONFIG custom mapping with GUID), custom gamecontrollerdb.txt entries for Steam virtual pads, or InputPlumber/uinput proxy devices with stable identities? (4) RECONNECTION: since bwrap bind-mounts of numbered /dev/input nodes are static, a mid-session disconnect+reconnect assigns a NEW eventN that is not bound in the sandbox — what are proven patterns for hot-replug survival: binding the whole /dev/input with SDL ignore/allow hints for isolation instead of node-level masking, udev-rule stable symlinks, a per-slot uinput forwarding proxy daemon on the host (e.g. like InputPlumber, moltengamepad, or a custom evdev→uinput relay) whose node persists across physical reconnects, passing /dev/uinput into the sandbox, or Steam Input rebinding behavior on reconnect? Prioritize: Controlify source on GitHub (controller discovery/naming code), SDL2/SDL3 Linux joystick backend source/docs (udev vs evdev enumeration, name/GUID derivation), Steam Input virtual gamepad documentation/VID:PID naming, bubblewrap device-binding semantics, and InputPlumber/similar proxy projects. Deliverable: a diagnosis of the misidentification, and a concrete recommended architecture for per-slot isolation + reconnect-proof controller nodes with proper identity that Controlify will map correctly.

confirmed.claim: Controlify uses SDL3 (not SDL2, GLFW-only, or direct evdev) as its primary controller backend, so controller names/GUIDs inside the bwrap sandbox are resolved by SDL3's platform joystick code, not by Minecraft's own input stack.
confirmed.quote: Controlify makes great use of SDL3 for handling controllers.

confirmed.claim: If SDL3 fails to load (or the user opts out of downloading the native), Controlify falls back to Minecraft's GLFW controller API, which has a different name/GUID resolution path — meaning identification behavior can differ depending on which backend is active in the sandbox.
confirmed.quote: If for whatever reason Controlify fails to load SDL or the user chooses not to download it, Controlify will fall back on the Minecraft-provided GLFW controller API.

confirmed.claim: Controlify retrieves the controller GUID via SDL_GetGamepadGUIDForID and the USB vendor/product IDs via SDL_GetGamepadVendor/SDL_GetGamepadProduct, so distinguishing a Steam virtual pad (vendor 28de) from a physical DS4 (054c) depends on SDL correctly reporting these IDs inside the sandbox.
confirmed.quote: protected SDL_JoystickGUID SDL_GetControllerGUIDForID(SDL_JoystickID jid) ... protected short SDL_GetControllerVendor(SDL_Gamepad ptrController) ... protected short SDL_GetControllerProduct(SDL_Gamepad ptrController)

confirmed.claim: Controlify's controller backend is SDL3, accessed through the author's own Java JNA bindings (dev.isxander.sdl3java); it does not read evdev or use GLFW as the primary path (GLFWControllerManager exists only as a fallback when SDL natives are not loaded). Therefore controller enumeration/naming behavior inside bwrap is determined by SDL3's Linux joystick backend, not by the mod itself.
confirmed.quote: Controlify supports _all_ controllers, thanks to its usage of the [SDL3](https://libsdl.org/) library, which is the most advanced cross-platform input library available. ... logger.debugLog("Controller manager using SDL3"); logger.validateIsTrue(SDLNativesLoader.isLoaded(), "SDL3 natives must be loaded before creating SDLControllerManager");

confirmed.claim: Controlify bundles its own gamecontrollerdb for SDL3 (src/main/resources/assets/controlify/controllers/gamecontrollerdb-sdl3.txt) and loads it at runtime via SDL_AddGamepadMappingsFromIO; friendly names and gamepad classification therefore depend on the device's SDL GUID matching an entry in this bundled database, and a custom mapping entry (e.g. for Steam's 28de virtual pad GUID) could be injected the same way.
confirmed.quote: Optional<Resource> resourceOpt = resourceProvider.getResource(CUtil.rl("controllers/gamecontrollerdb-sdl3.txt")); ... int count = SDL_AddGamepadMappingsFromIO(stream, true); if (count < 0) { CUtil.LOGGER.error("Failed to load gamepad mappings: {}", SDL_GetError()); }

confirmed.claim: Controlify supports hotplug purely through SDL3's event stream — it polls SDL_PollEvent each tick and reacts to SDL_EVENT_JOYSTICK_ADDED / SDL_EVENT_JOYSTICK_REMOVED — so mid-session reconnect survival inside bwrap depends entirely on whether SDL3's Linux backend can detect the newly created device node in the sandbox (e.g. via inotify on /dev/input when udev is unavailable).

confirmed.claim: SDL's Linux joystick backend derives controller GUIDs from the device name string (in src/joystick/linux/SDL_sysjoystick.c), so when SDL commit 4d5949d switched Steam Virtual Gamepads to a single hardcoded name, all virtual gamepad instances began producing identical GUIDs — meaning the name a game like Controlify sees for a Steam virtual pad is set by SDL itself, not read from the kernel evdev node.
confirmed.quote: After commit 4d5949d, SDL switched to a hardcoded name string for Steam Virtual Gamepad controllers. This eliminated the uniqueness of generated GUIDs that previously included instance numbers. ... The issue requests GUID modification to restore per-instance uniqueness ... in SDL/src/joystick/linux/SDL_sysjoystick.c at line 464.

confirmed.claim: Before that change, Steam Input virtual gamepads enumerated in SDL on Linux with per-slot numbered names 'Microsoft X-Box 360 pad 0', 'Microsoft X-Box 360 pad 1', etc., each yielding a distinct GUID — confirming that 'Microsoft X-Box 360 pad' is the expected identity of a Steam virtual uinput device, and that a game reporting 'Wireless Controller' is seeing the physical DS4 node rather than the Steam virtual node.
confirmed.quote: Previously, devices used a numbered pattern like "Microsoft X-Box 360 pad 0", "pad 1", etc., which generated unique GUIDs.

confirmed.claim: Steam Virtual Gamepads carry vendor ID 0x28de and product ID 0x11ff, and under the hardcoded-name behavior all instances share the SDL GUID 03006983de280000ff11000001000000, so a VID:PID (28de:11ff) match is a more robust way to identify/allowlist a Steam virtual pad per slot than GUID or name matching.
confirmed.quote: GUID: 03006983de280000ff11000001000000 ... Device: Steam Virtual Gamepad ... Hardware: Vendor ID 0x28de, Product ID 0x11ff

confirmed.claim: In the non-udev fallback path SDL handles hotplug by watching /dev/input with inotify (IN_CREATE|IN_DELETE|IN_MOVE|IN_ATTRIB), and if inotify is unavailable it re-scans /dev/input every 3 seconds gated on the directory's mtime changing — meaning a reconnected controller is only picked up if its new eventN node actually appears inside the sandbox's /dev/input, which static bwrap per-node binds prevent.

refuted.claim: The controller name Controlify displays is the driver-provided name surfaced through a DriverNameComponent, so a 'Wireless Controller' label indicates the underlying driver (SDL3 or GLFW) reported the physical DS4 evdev name rather than Controlify renaming anything itself.

Controlify's gamepad driver identifies controllers via native SDL3 calls made through the Java JNA binding library 'sdl3java' (dev.isxander.sdl3java), not via GLFW, hidapi, or direct evdev access — so controller identity inside a sandbox is entirely determined by SDL3's Linux joystick backend behavior.

The controller's displayed name comes directly from SDL3's SDL_GetGamepadName() with no additional fallback or renaming logic in this driver, meaning whatever name SDL resolves for the device node (e.g. the evdev name 'Wireless Controller' when udev data/mapping lookup fails) is what Controlify shows.

The controller name Controlify displays is fetched directly from SDL3: SDLControllerManager.getControllerSystemName() returns SDL_GetGamepadNameForID(jid) when SDL classifies the device as a gamepad, otherwise SDL_GetJoystickNameForID(jid). So seeing the DS4's evdev name "Wireless Controller" means SDL is enumerating the physical DS4 node (or failing gamepad-mapping/GUID resolution), not a Controlify naming bug.

unverified.claim: SDL automatically disables libudev-based joystick enumeration when it detects it is running inside a sandbox/container (which includes Flatpak-style bwrap environments detected via SDL_GetSandbox), falling back to direct /dev/input scanning — so missing /run/udev inside bwrap does not itself break enumeration; SDL never consults the udev database in that case.

The controller name SDL reports comes from kernel ioctls on the opened /dev/input node — EVIOCGNAME for the product string and EVIOCGID for vendor/product — combined via SDL_CreateJoystickName; therefore a game seeing "Wireless Controller" (the DS4 kernel evdev name) means SDL opened the physical DS4 node, not the Steam virtual "Microsoft X-Box 360 pad" uinput node.

SDL's Linux joystick backend disables udev-based enumeration when it detects it is running in a container (Flatpak or pressure-vessel), because udev netlink event notification is unreliable under user namespaces.

SDL's container detection is purely filesystem-based: it checks only for the paths /.flatpak-info and /run/host/container-manager, so a plain bwrap sandbox that does not expose either marker will NOT trigger the container fallback and SDL will still attempt udev enumeration.

When a container is detected, SDL falls back to inotify-based joystick enumeration (ENUMERATION_FALLBACK) instead of udev, i.e., it watches /dev/input directly rather than relying on libudev device metadata.

SDL's udev-based joystick hotplug notification does not work reliably inside containers that use user namespaces (such as Flatpak and Steam's pressure-vessel), because udev event notification is delivered over netlink, which user namespaces break.
SDL has an alternative, inotify-based joystick detection fallback (introduced in SDL Bug #5337), and this issue proposed automatically switching to it instead of udev when a container is detected.

SDL's automatic fallback to non-udev joystick detection is triggered only by specific container markers: the existence of the file /.flatpak-info (Flatpak) or the directory /run/pressure-vessel (Steam Linux Runtime) — meaning a plain bwrap sandbox that provides neither marker will not automatically switch SDL off the udev backend.

SDL's standard/recommended Linux builds use udev for joystick detection; the official README treats a udev-enabled SDL as the expected configuration, meaning joystick discovery and classification on Linux normally flows through udev rather than raw /dev/input scanning.

Under the udev backend, SDL only treats a /dev/input/eventXX device as a joystick if udev has tagged it with the property ID_INPUT_JOYSTICK=1; devices lacking that udev metadata are not picked up as joysticks, which requires a udev rule to force. This implies SDL depends on udev's device metadata database (unavailable when /run/udev is not bound into a sandbox) for correct controller enumeration/classification.
